Oshawa is the city that Canada's auto industry built. For more than a century it was the home of General Motors of Canada, and the McLaughlin family's Parkwood Estate — a National Historic Site once home to GM founder R.S. McLaughlin — still stands as a reminder of that legacy. With 175,383 residents in 2021, Oshawa anchors the eastern end of the Greater Toronto Area on the Lake Ontario shore, about 50 km from downtown Toronto, at the head of Durham Region.

Simcoe Street forms the city's spine, running from the Lakeview Park waterfront north through a walkable downtown of brick century homes and past Ontario Tech University and Durham College to newer ground. Older neighbourhoods near downtown and the McLaughlin area hold early-1900s houses on tight lots, while postwar bungalows fill the south and east near the GM plant lands. North Oshawa, above Taunton Road and Conlin Road, has grown quickly with new subdivisions and townhomes serving the student and commuter population around the universities. Owen Sound is the commercial, health and government hub for Grey and Bruce counties, with the Owen Sound hospital, county offices, retail and a base of manufacturing and marine and agricultural services.
